quote: A man that will take back a move at chess will pick a pocket.
The blunders are all there on the board, waiting to be made.
When you see a good move, look for a better one.
You must take your opponent into a deep dark forest where 2+2=5, and the path leading out is only wide enough for one.
Pawns are the soul of chess.
The hardest part of chess is winning a won game.
A bad plan is better than none at all.
The threat is stronger than the execution.
Without error there can be no brilliancy.
Chess is war over the board. The object is to crush the opponent’s mind.
That’s what chess is all about. One day you give your opponent a lesson, the next day he gives you one.
Chess is life.
The older I grow, the more I value pawns.
The winner of the game is the player who makes the next-to-last mistake.
It is always better to sacrifice your opponent’s men.
No one ever won a game by resigning.
The beauty of a move lies not in its appearance but in the thought behind it.
The passed pawn is a criminal, who should be kept under lock and key.
Chess, like love, like music, has the power to make men happy.
One bad move nullifies forty good ones.
Every chess master was once a beginner.
Even the laziest king flees wildly in the face of a double check.
The pin is mightier than the sword.
The knight on the rim is dim.
If your opponent offers you a draw, try to work out why he thinks he’s worse off.
Chess is a sea in which a gnat may drink and an elephant may bathe.
I have never in my life played the French Defence, which is the dullest of all openings.
There are two types of sacrifices: correct ones, and mine.
Later, I began to succeed in decisive games. Perhaps because I realized a very simple truth: not only was I worried, but also my opponent.
Some sacrifices are sound; the rest are mine.
When you don’t know what to do, improve your worst-placed piece.
To play for a draw, at any rate with White, is to some degree a crime against chess.
In chess, at least, the brave inherit the earth.
Chess is 99 percent tactics.
Tactics flow from a superior position.
The good player is always lucky.
You may learn much more from a game you lose than from a game you win.
In order to improve your game, you must study the endgame before everything else.
Chess is a very logical game and it is the man who can reason most logically and profoundly in it that ought to win.
A passed pawn increases in strength as the number of pieces on the board diminishes.
The winning of a pawn among good players of even strength often means the winning of the game.
Only the player with the initiative has the right to attack.
Capture of the adverse king is the ultimate but not the first object of the game.
The king is a fighting piece. Use it!
The middlegame is chess itself.
The most powerful weapon in chess is to have the next move.
If you are going to make a bad move, make it quickly.
To lose one’s objective attitude to a position nearly always means ruining your game.
Before the endgame, the gods have placed the middlegame.
When you find mate in one, look for mate in two.
A plan is made for a few moves only, not for the whole game.
The task of the positional player is systematically to accumulate slight advantages.
Nothing so easily ruins a position as pawn moves.
It is not enough to be a good player; you must also play well.
Many have become chess masters; no one has become the master of chess.
He who fears an isolated queen’s pawn should give up chess.
Chess is eminently and emphatically the philosopher’s game.
Help your pieces so they can help you.
Combinations are the poetry of the game.
The pleasure to be derived from a chess combination lies in the feeling that a human mind is behind the game.
The main thing that develops positional judgment is detailed analytical work and a self-critical attitude to your games.
It is better to follow a plan consistently, even if it is not the best one, than to play without a plan at all.
During a chess tournament a master must envisage himself as a cross between an ascetic monk and a beast of prey.
Chess first of all teaches you to be objective.
I believe that true beauty in chess is attained only by a game in which both sides play perfectly.
The fact that a player is very short of time is no reason for his opponent to play quickly.
The only thing chess players have in common is chess.
Good positions don’t win games; good moves do.
When your house is on fire, you can’t be bothered with the neighbors.
You need not play well — just help your opponent to play badly.
I often play a move I know how to refute.
Chess is a cold bath for the mind.
Chess is imagination.
The most important feature of the chess position is the activity of the pieces.
Whoever sees no other aim in the game than checkmate will never become a good chess player.
The defensive power of a pinned piece is only imaginary.
First restrain, then blockade, finally destroy.
The isolated pawn casts gloom over the whole chessboard.
A knight on the sixth rank is like a bone in the throat.
Lies and hypocrisy do not survive for long on the chessboard.
Education in chess has to be an education in independent thinking and judging.
A good sacrifice is one that is not necessarily sound but leaves your opponent dazed and confused.
If chess is a science, it is too inexact to be a science. If chess is an art, it is too exact to be an art. If chess is a game, it is too serious to be a game.
Chess is a fairy tale of 1,001 blunders.
When I am White, I win because I am White. When I am Black, I win because I am Bogoljubov.
I don’t believe in psychology. I believe in good moves.
The stomach is an essential part of the chess master.
Chess is ruthless: you’ve got to be prepared to kill people.
A chess game is divided into three stages: the first, when you hope you have the advantage; the second, when you believe you have an advantage; and the third, when you know you’re going to lose.
